subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Applies triangle angle sum (180°) to quadrilaterals
explanation: Student believes the interior angles of any polygon sum to 180°, applying the triangle rule universally.
why_students_think_this: The triangle angle sum of 180° is one of the most-practiced geometry facts. Students overgeneralise it to all polygons without learning the general formula (n−2) × 180°.
grade8_impact: All quadrilateral angle problems will use the wrong total, causing systematic errors.
grade12_impact: Will affect polygon interior/exterior angle problems in higher geometry.
root_cause: Overgeneralisation of triangle angle sum; has not learned the general polygon angle sum formula

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Uses an arbitrary or incorrect total for quadrilateral angles
explanation: Uses a value other than 360° or 180° as the total angle sum, indicating no stable knowledge of the polygon angle sum property.
why_students_think_this: No reliable rule has been internalized. Student may be guessing or confusing with exterior angle sums.
grade8_impact: Cannot solve any problem requiring angle sum of a quadrilateral.
grade12_impact: Weak polygon angle reasoning will affect all higher geometry.
root_cause: No stable knowledge of polygon angle sum properties

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Applies pentagon angle sum (540°) to quadrilateral
explanation: Uses the angle sum for a 5-sided polygon (540°) when working with a 4-sided polygon.
why_students_think_this: Has learned the general formula (n−2)×180° but applied n = 5 instead of n = 4, likely confusing quadrilateral with pentagon.
grade8_impact: Minor — likely a counting error, not a conceptual gap in the formula.
grade12_impact: Negligible if the formula is understood.
root_cause: Off-by-one error in counting sides, not a conceptual misunderstanding

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Overgeneralises rectangle properties to all quadrilaterals
explanation: Assumes that all quadrilaterals (or all parallelograms) must have right angles, area computed as length × breadth, and equal diagonals.
why_students_think_this: Rectangles and squares dominate early geometry exposure (classroom walls, books, screens). These become the default mental model for 'four-sided shape'.
grade8_impact: Cannot work correctly with parallelograms, rhombuses, or trapeziums. Errors in classification and area.
grade12_impact: Coordinate geometry, transformations, and vector problems all involve non-rectangular parallelograms.
root_cause: Insufficient exposure to non-rectangular quadrilaterals in early geometry

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Thinks all parallelograms have equal sides (confuses with rhombus)
explanation: Attributes the equal-all-sides property of a rhombus to all parallelograms.
why_students_think_this: Both shapes look similar in diagrams. The defining difference (opposite-equal vs all-equal sides) is subtle and often not taught with enough contrast.
grade8_impact: Incorrect property application in parallelogram problems — uses rhombus area formula or equal side lengths.
grade12_impact: Vector representation and dot products depend on distinguishing parallelogram types.
root_cause: Insufficient differentiation between parallelogram and rhombus during classification teaching

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Confuses diagonal properties across different quadrilaterals
explanation: Applies diagonal properties (equal diagonals, perpendicular diagonals, bisecting diagonals) to the wrong shape — e.g., assumes all parallelograms have equal diagonals like rectangles.
why_students_think_this: Multiple diagonal properties exist across multiple shapes. Without a clear property-shape mapping, students mix them up.
grade8_impact: Errors in diagonal-based area calculations (rhombus, kite) and length problems.
grade12_impact: Coordinate geometry problems involving diagonals will be affected.
root_cause: Properties are memorized without a systematic shape-property framework

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Confuses co-interior angles with alternate or opposite angles
explanation: Treats co-interior angles (which sum to 180°) as equal instead of supplementary, or confuses them with alternate interior angles (which are equal).
why_students_think_this: Multiple angle relationships exist for parallel lines. Students who haven't consolidated these relationships default to 'angles are equal'.
grade8_impact: Errors in trapezium and parallelogram angle problems where the 'sum to 180°' rule applies.
grade12_impact: Proof-based problems in geometry require precise angle relationship knowledge.
root_cause: Co-interior, alternate, and corresponding angle rules are taught together and often confused

subject: quadrilaterals
explanation: Thinks rectangles have all four sides equal, confusing rectangle with square.
why_students_think_this: Squares appear frequently in examples. Students who have only seen square examples of rectangles may not differentiate.
grade8_impact: Errors in rectangle area and perimeter problems where length ≠ breadth.
grade12_impact: Minor, but reflects a gap in the shape-property hierarchy.
root_cause: Overgeneralisation from square examples to all rectangles

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Thinks square is the only quadrilateral with all sides equal
explanation: Does not know that a rhombus also has all sides equal. Treats the equal-all-sides property as unique to squares.
why_students_think_this: Square is the most familiar equal-sided quadrilateral. Rhombus is taught later and less often.
grade8_impact: Cannot classify or work with rhombuses correctly.
grade12_impact: Minimal long-term impact once rhombus is properly introduced.
root_cause: Limited exposure to rhombus; square dominates 'equal-sides' mental model

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Does not know the defining property of a trapezium
explanation: Cannot identify a trapezium by its one-pair-parallel-sides property. Confuses it with other quadrilaterals.
why_students_think_this: Trapezium is typically introduced last among quadrilaterals and gets the least practice time.
grade8_impact: Cannot correctly identify, classify, or find area of trapeziums.
grade12_impact: Area under a curve (trapezoidal rule) requires understanding trapezium geometry.
root_cause: Insufficient practice with trapezium problems compared to other quadrilaterals

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Thinks rectangle is a special type of rhombus
explanation: Reverses the actual hierarchy: believes rectangle is a subset of rhombus rather than a subset of parallelogram.
why_students_think_this: Knows both are parallelograms but incorrectly uses the equal-sides criterion to order them.
grade8_impact: Cannot correctly state which properties follow from classification.
grade12_impact: Geometric proof and deduction will be affected by incorrect hierarchical reasoning.
root_cause: Has memorized shape names but not the inclusion relationships between them

subject: quadrilaterals
explanation: Uses a perimeter formula (sum of sides) when asked for area, or vice versa.
why_students_think_this: Area and perimeter are both numerical properties of shapes introduced around the same time. Without clear conceptual distinction, formulas get mixed up.
grade8_impact: Systematic errors on all geometry problems involving measurement.
grade12_impact: Foundational — affects all subsequent area/volume/surface area problems.
root_cause: Area and perimeter not conceptually distinguished — both seen as 'measuring a shape'

subject: quadrilaterals
misconception_name: Halves the parallelogram area (applies triangle formula)
explanation: Uses (base × height) / 2 for a parallelogram instead of base × height. Applies the triangle area formula.
why_students_think_this: The triangle area formula (½ × base × height) is very similar to the parallelogram formula. Students who haven't differentiated them apply ½ universally.
grade8_impact: All parallelogram and rhombus area answers will be half the correct value.
grade12_impact: Vector cross product area (parallelogram) will be incorrectly halved.
root_cause: Triangle and parallelogram area formulas not clearly differentiated; ½ is over-applied
